title: Invisible Injections: Unicode-based Adversarial Attacks on Tool-Integrated LLM Agents

original.abstract:
As large language model (LLM) agents increasingly integrate sophisticated tool- use capabilities, their security boundaries are facing mounting challenges. Existing research on prompt injection has largely focused on overt textual perturbations, which are often readily detected by human reviewers or intercepted by rule- based filters. This paper introduces a new attack paradigm, Unicode- based Stealthy Injection (USI). USI exploits the perception- semantic gap between the human visual system and LLM tokenization: by crafting visually imperceptible or deceptive Unicode character sequences—such as Zero- width characters, invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters—an attacker can inject malicious payloads into an agent while preserving the apparent visual consistency of the instruction.  

We systematically define a threat model targeting tool- integrated agents, encompassing two attack vectors: prompt- marketplace poisoning and man- in- the- middle tool hijacking. By inserting or substituting specific Unicode sequences within instructions, an attacker can induce information leakage, denial of service (DoS), and content manipulation. We conduct extensive empirical evaluations across six mainstream agent frameworks (e.g., Claude Desktop, Cursor, Augment) and multiple state- of- the- art models. The results indicate that widely deployed models generally lack effective defenses against this class of attacks. For instance, a DoS attack leveraging Zero- width characters can cause system response latency to grow exponentially; semantic hijacking based on homoglyphs and hidden tags achieves an attack success rate (ASR) exceeding \(85\%\) on certain models.  

To mitigate this threat, we further propose a context- aware hybrid detection framework that combines a Unicode- oriented rulebased scanning engine with a fine- tuned semantic verification model. To improve robustness and reduce false positives, we construct a synthetic dataset containing both malicious injection samples and benign Unicode perturbations (negative samples) for fine- tuning. Finally, we perform a large- scale "in- the- wild" evaluation on 54,355 real- world instructions, successfully identifying 74 stealthy injection prompts with substantive harmful impact, thereby validating both the stealthiness of USI in practical settings and the effectiveness of our detection framework.
original.introduction:
Do x and x look the same to you? Visually, the characters x and x are nearly indistinguishable to human observers, yet most Natural Language Processing (NLP) systems can accurately differentiate between them. In fact, x and x belong to the Latin and Cyrillic character sets respectively; though they are visually homoglyphic characters, they fall under distinct Unicode encoding systems. Similarly, visual discrepancies can occur not just in character shape, but in string length. Consider the character count of the string "123": its actual length can reach 100 characters. This is because the string contains 97 Zero- width non- joiners following the three visible digits. Beyond zero- width non- joiners, Unicode tag characters and bidirectional text control characters (BIDI) also fall into this category of special characters, whose definitions and properties are elaborated in the official Unicode Standard[15][16]. These easily overlooked properties of Unicode characters are not trivial technical details, but are directly associated with the security risks of Large Language Models (LLMs). By design, such Unicode characters were intended to address specific text processing needs, including multilingual text typesetting and attribute annotation. However, relevant studies[17] [10] have confirmed that they can be maliciously repurposed and embedded into LLM prompts to construct adversarial suffixes that are undetectable to the human eye. Notably, while these characters are visually imperceptible, they occupy additional text space during the encoding process of the model tokenizer, thereby interfering with the model's processing logic. Security threats based on Unicode characters not only affect basic NLP models, but also extend to agent systems centered on LLMs. Currently, LLMs are being widely integrated into agent frameworks[38] [26], leveraging tool invocation capabilities to perform various complex tasks. Meanwhile, a growing number of agents are deployed in environments with access to user privacy data or directly connected to real- world business systems.[9] This trend has introduced <--- Page Split ---> ![](images/1_0.jpg)
Figure 1: Prompt reuse in on-line communities
new security challenges. Direct prompt injection [30] [47] is a typical method to achieve such attacks: attackers input malicious instructions into LLM applications to overwrite or tamper with the system prompts preset by developers, thereby inducing the model to perform actions that deviate from its intended design. Specifically, attackers can hijack agents to steal users' sensitive data, or force them to execute unauthorized operations such as redirecting to phishing websites and running malicious commands [46]. Explicit attack methods, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) [47] and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) [11], generate adversarial suffixes that either manifest as nonsensical garbled text or expose malicious intent through semantically readable prompts, making them susceptible to human inspection. Conversely, while implicit attacks utilizing Unicode perturbations [10, 14] offer greater stealth, they have primarily been evaluated on static NLP tasks (e.g., translation) rather than the dynamic, multiturn interaction context of tool- integrated agents. Consequently, a critical research gap remains: existing strategies either fail to conceal adversarial suffixes from human observation, or are limited to static language models, thereby overlooking the unique vulnerabilities of tool- integrated agents. To address the challenge of stealthiness, we shifted our research focus to the intrinsic text processing mechanisms of LLMs. We identified a fundamental "perception- semantic gap" between human visual rendering and the model's tokenization process. Specifically, certain Unicode characters (such as Unicode tag or control characters) are not only "transparent" or highly deceptive to the human eye but are simultaneously parsed as distinct tokens with explicit, independent semantics from the perspective of the model's tokenizer. This discovery reveals that attackers can achieve a complete decoupling of "visual presentation" from "execution logic." Particularly in the context of emerging tool- integrated agents, this decoupling transcends mere text generation errors; it enables attackers to directly hijack the agent's tool invocation by injecting "invisible" or "spoofed" instructions without the user's awareness. Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel attack paradigm termed "Unicode- based Stealthy Injection" (USI). This paradigm exploits the fundamental discrepancy between the human visual perception system and the LLM tokenization mechanism. By meticulously crafting prompt payloads containing specific Unicode sequences—such as Unicode tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional text control characters—we conceal malicious semantic instructions beneath the guise of benign text. This approach establishes a "perception- semantic gap": while the input remains visually stealthy and innocuous, it is decoded within the LLM's embedding space as high- priority malicious commands. Consequently, this induces agents to execute unintended tool calls, achieving a covert system hijacking. Utilizing our detection framework, we identified and verified 74 instructions with substantive malicious intent from this massive dataset. ## 2 Background ### 2.1 Unicode and LLM Representation The Unicode Standard provides a unified character encoding framework designed to represent writing systems, symbols, and notational forms used across the world. It assigns each character a unique numerical identifier, known as a code point (e.g., U+0061 for the Latin letter "a"), and defines how these code points are serialized into byte sequences through encoding schemes such as UTF- 8. The visual form presented to users, referred to as the glyph, is determined by the text rendering engine and may differ from the underlying logical representation. In this study, we consider several classes of Unicode character features that are commonly supported by modern text- processing and rendering systems, yet exhibit subtle distinctions between logical encoding and visual presentation: - Homoglyphs. Homoglyphs are characters that have distinct Unicode code points but render as identical or nearly indistinguishable glyphs. Such characters may originate from different writing systems while appearing visually equivalent. For example, the Latin letter "a" (U+0061) and the Cyrillic letter "a" (U+0430) are visually identical in most common typefaces, despite representing different characters at the encoding level. The Unicode Consortium documents these relationships through dedicated confusables data files and technical reports. - Zero-Width Characters (ZWC). Unicode defines a set of invisible characters intended to support fine-grained typographic control, such as the Zero-Width Space (U+200B) and the Zero-Width Joiner (U+200D). These characters do not produce visible glyphs when rendered, yet they occupy positions in the logical character stream and may influence text segmentation, shaping, or layout behavior. - Tag Characters. The Unicode block ranging from U+E0000 to U+E007F is reserved for Tag characters, originally designed to encode auxiliary information such as language tags. Although these characters are typically suppressed by modern rendering engines, they remain part of the underlying text stream. Unlike zero-width characters, Tag characters can be systematically mapped to corresponding ASCII characters through a defined offset, giving them explicit symbolic meaning. - Bidirectional (BIDI) Control Characters. To support right-to-left writing systems such as Arabic and Hebrew, Unicode specifies the Bidirectional Algorithm (UBA), which relies on special control characters (e.g., Right-to-Left Override, U+202E) to influence the visual ordering of text. These controls modify how text is displayed on screen while preserving the original logical order of characters in memory. While the Unicode character features summarized above are defined at the level of text encoding and rendering, they directly shape how textual input is processed by LLM. Modern LLM pipelines typically operate on normalized text that is subsequently transformed into subword or token representations. However, this transformation does not eliminate the distinctions between logical character sequences and their visual appearance. Instead, these distinctions are often preserved—or even amplified, during tokenization, embedding, and attention-based processing. As a result, characters that differ at the code- point level but share identical glyphs, as well as characters that are visually absent yet logically present in the text stream, can lead to divergent internal representations within the model. Understanding these character- level properties is therefore a prerequisite for analyzing how LLMs interpret, compare, and reason over text that appears visually similar to human users. In the remainder of this section, we build upon this characterization to examine how LLM text processing stages handle such Unicode features in practice. ### 2.2 Text Processing in LLM-CentricJR: (LLM-based) Agent Building upon the character- level distinctions discussed above, we now examine how such properties propagate through LLM- centric agent architectures. Modern LLM- based agents extend beyond passive text generation by integrating external tools and APIs, enabling the model to parse instructions, plan actions, and trigger ![](images/3_0.jpg)
Figure 2: Handling of Unicode Characters by LLMs
concrete operations in response to user inputs. In this architecture, the LLM functions as the decision- making core, mediating between raw textual input and downstream execution. Unlike traditional static NLP pipelines, agent systems operate in dynamic, multi- turn environments and routinely process inputs originating from untrusted users or external tools. As shown in Figure 3, the input processing pipeline (beginning with raw Unicode text and continuing through tokenization and representation learning) plays a central role in shaping the agent's subsequent reasoning and behavior. Importantly, any discrepancy between how text appears to human users and how it is internally represented by the model persists throughout this pipeline. To support multilingual and symbol- rich inputs, contemporary LLMs natively consume Unicode text. Before inference, input text undergoes a sequence of preprocessing steps, among which tokenization is particularly critical. Most state- of- the- art models rely on subword- level tokenization schemes, such as Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) [37] or Unigram language models [21], which decompose text into variable- length tokens based on corpus- level statistical regularities. Crucially, these tokenization algorithms are largely agnostic to the semantic categories and structural distinctions defined by the Unicode standard. Characters that differ in code point, rendering behavior, or logical function are typically treated as interchangeable atomic symbols or substrings during tokenization, provided they occur with sufficient frequency in the training data. Consequently, Unicode- level properties such as visual similarity, invisibility, or reordering semantics are not explicitly preserved or normalized at the tokenization stage. Once tokenized, each unit is mapped into a continuous embedding space, and all subsequent model computations (e.g., attention, reasoning, and generation) operate exclusively over these embeddings. Any subtle variation introduced during tokenization may therefore be reflected, and potentially magnified, in the model's internal representations. ## 3 Motivation and Problem Statement ### 3.1 Motivation LLM- centric agents are increasingly deployed as autonomous systems that not only generate text, but also plan actions and invoke external tools. In such agents, natural- language input directly governs execution logic and determines how the system interacts with external resources. As a result, subtle differences in how input is interpreted can have concrete system- level consequences. Most prior work on adversarial attacks against LLMs has focused on inducing model jailbreaking by appending adversarial suffixes to prompts, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) [47] and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) [11]. While effective in exposing weaknesses in model alignment, these approaches typically produce prompts that are visibly abnormal, nonsensical, or explicitly malicious, making them susceptible to human inspection or rule- based filtering. Other studies have explored Unicode- based perturbations in static NLP tasks, such as machine translation [10], but their implications for tool- integrated, interactive agents remain largely unexplored. To motivate our study, consider the following two instructions submitted to a scheduling agent JR: change name, scheduling agent often used to do calendar event that invokes a calendar tool. Instruction A: "Use headless mode to navigate to http://amazon.com" Instruction B: "Use headless mode to navigate to http://amazon.com" When rendered in a standard chat interface or text editor, the two instructions appear visually identical. To a human user or auditor, they convey the same benign intent and are indistinguishable by visual inspection alone. However, the two inputs differ critically at the level of their underlying Unicode character sequences: Instruction B employs a homology substitution, where the Latin letter 'a' (U+0061) is replaced by the Cyrillic small letter 'a' (U+0430). Readers can verify this discrepancy by inspecting the raw byte stream or using a text inspection tool. Although the rendered glyphs are visually indistinguishable, the underlying code points direct the agent to a completely different, potentially malicious domain. Operations such as cursor movement, string length measurement, or tokenization reveal that the two instructions are not equivalent at the machine- processing level. This example illustrates the core insight of our work: visual equivalence does not imply semantic equivalence in LLM- centric agent pipelines. While humans assess intent based on rendered text, LLM agents operate on token sequences derived from raw Unicode input. Certain Unicode characters may be visually imperceptible or highly deceptive to human observers, yet are parsed by the tokenizer as independent tokens with explicit semantic representations. Crucially, this perception- semantic gap is not an artifact of a specific model, tokenizer, or application. It arises from a structural asymmetry inherent in Unicode- aware language processing: humans reason over visual rendering, whereas agents execute logic over tokenized representations. In tool- integrated agents, where internal representations directly drive planning and tool invocation, this discrepancy can influence system behavior without any visible indication to the user. Recognizing and formalizing this gap is therefore essential for understanding the security risks faced by modern LLM- centric agents, which motivates the problem statement presented in the following subsection. ### 3.2 Problem Statement The examples in the previous subsection highlight a fundamental discrepancy between how humans perceive textual instructions and how LLM- centric agents interpret and execute them. While individual instances of such discrepancies may appear anecdotal, they point to a broader and largely unexamined problem space: the systematic exploitation of Unicode- level ambiguities in tool- integrated LLM agents. In this work, we seek to move beyond isolated demonstrations and conduct a comprehensive study of how Unicode character properties interact with the input processing pipelines of LLM- centric agents. Our goal is not merely to show that visually indistinguishable inputs can lead to different internal representations, but to understand the scope, mechanisms, and practical implications of this phenomenon across real- world agent frameworks and usage scenarios. Specifically, we aim to answer the following high- level questions. First, what classes of Unicode characters can introduce discrepancies between visual appearance and model interpretation in agent pipelines? Second, how do such discrepancies propagate through tokenization, embedding, and reasoning stages to influence agent behavior, particularly in the presence of tool invocation? Third, to what extent are contemporary, widely deployed LLM agents susceptible to such inputs in realistic settings? Beyond characterizing the problem, we also seek principled mitigation strategies. Rather than proposing ad hoc defenses for individual cases, our objective is to design a detection framework that systematically identifies Unicode- induced discrepancies that are likely to alter an agent's execution semantics. Such a framework must balance precision and recall, distinguishing benign uses of Unicode characters from inputs that materially affect agent behavior. Finally, to ground our study in practice, we aim to evaluate both the prevalence and impact of these issues in real- world data. By analyzing large- scale corpora of instructions collected from public repositories and agent ecosystems, we assess how frequently such Unicode- induced discrepancies arise in the wild and whether they pose tangible risks in deployed systems. ## 4 Ghost Prompt Injection (GPI) Attacks ### 4.1 Threat Model We consider an adversary who seeks to manipulate the behavior of a tool- integrated LLM agent by crafting inputs that exploit discrepancies between human visual perception and the agent's internal text processing pipeline. The victims are end users and the LLM- centric agents they interact with, particularly agents that invoke external tools to perform actions on the user's behalf. The attacker's objective is to induce unintended or unauthorized agent behavior while keeping the input visually benign and indistinguishable to human observers. We assume the attacker does not have access to the model's internal parameters, training data, or system prompts, and operates in a black- box setting. The attacker's capabilities are limited to controlling or influencing textual inputs provided to the agent, including prompts authored by third parties or data returned by external tools. As shown in Figure 1, a representative attack scenario arises from prompt reuse in online communities. A user searches the web for high- quality prompt templates to improve productivity, such as prompts for scheduling meetings, drafting emails, or automating workflows. The adversary publishes a seemingly benign prompt on a public forum or repository, advertising it as an effective template. When the user copies and pastes this prompt into their agent, the instruction appears visually normal and consistent with the advertised functionality. However, the underlying text contains hidden discrepancies that are not visible during inspection. As a result, the agent processes a different internal representation than what the user perceives, leading to unintended behavior during planning or tool invocation, without any explicit indication to the user that the prompt has been manipulated. ### 4.2 Attack Formalization Let \(\mathcal{L}\) denote an LLM- centricJR: LLM- based or LLM- centric, pick one agent equipped with a set of external tools \(\tau\) . Given an input instruction \(I\) , the agent processes the raw Unicode text by applying a tokenizer, producing a discrete token sequence \[T = \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I), \quad (1)\] which serves as the sole basis for subsequent embedding, reasoning, planning, and tool invocation. In contrast, a human user \(U\) issuing an instruction \(I_{u}\) to invoke a tool \(T_{u}\in \tau\) does not reason over token sequences. Instead, the user relies on a rendering function \(R(\cdot)\) to perceive the instruction visually as \[V = R(I_{u}). \quad (2)\] The user's understanding and trust in the instruction are therefore grounded entirely in its rendered appearance. The core vulnerability arises from a mismatch between visual rendering and tokenization. Specifically, there exist pairs of instructions \((I_{\mathrm{benign}},I_{\mathrm{malicious}})\) that are visually indistinguishable to human users, yet semantically distinct to the agent: \[R(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\approx R(I_{\mathrm{malicious}}), \quad (3)\] while producing different token sequences under the agent's tokenizer: \[\mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\neq \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{malicious}}). \quad (4)\] As a result, although the user perceives both instructions as equivalent, the agent operates on different internal representations. Based on this vulnerability, we define the Ghost Injection attack paradigm. In the attack, an adversary crafts an instruction \(I_{\mathrm{malicious}}\) by embedding malicious commands into the Unicode- level representation of an otherwise benign instruction \(I_{u}\) . These embedded components remain invisible or visually indistinguishable to the user, yet are preserved in the token sequence processed by \(\mathcal{L}\) . As a result, the agent may be induced to perform unintended or unauthorized tool invocations, despite the user observing no deviation from the expected instruction. ### 4.3 Attack Taxonomy We classify Ghost Injection attacks according to the fundamental character- level transformations applied to a visually benign instruction. Let \(I_{\mathrm{benign}}\) denote the instruction as perceived by the user, and let \(I_{\mathrm{injected}}\) denote the instruction processed by the agent. divergence at the tokenization level: \[R(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\approx R(I_{\mathrm{injected}}),\quad \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{benign}})\neq \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{injected}}). \quad (5)\] Based on this criterion, we identify two fundamental classes of Invisible Injection attacks: insertion and modification. - Insertion-Based Invisible Injection. Insertion-based attacks inject additional Unicode characters into an otherwise benign instruction without altering its rendered appearance. Formally, let \(I_{\mathrm{benign}} = c_1c_2\dots c_n\) be a sequence of Unicode characters. The adversary constructs \[I_{\mathrm{injected}} = c_1\dots c_i\parallel \Delta \parallel c_{i + 1}\dots c_n, \quad (6)\] where \(\Delta\) is a sequence of Unicode characters that is visually suppressed or indistinguishable under \(R(\cdot)\) . Although \(\Delta\) does not affect visual perception, it introduces additional tokens that alter the agent's internal representation and subsequent execution semantics. - Substitution-Based Invisible Injection. Modification-based attacks replace one or more characters in the instruction with visually confusable alternatives. Let \(\phi (\cdot)\) denote a substitution function that maps selected characters to visually equivalent Unicode characters. The injected instruction is given by \[I_{\mathrm{injected}} = \phi (I_{\mathrm{benign}}), \quad (7)\] such that \[\begin{array}{r}R(\phi (I_{\mathrm{benign}}))\approx R(I_{\mathrm{benign}}),\\ \mathrm{Tokenizer}(\phi (I_{\mathrm{benign}}))\neq \mathrm{Tokenizer}(I_{\mathrm{benign}}). \end{array} \quad (8)\] Although the rendered text remains unchanged, the underlying code points differ, yielding distinct tokenization outcomes. Please note that we do not treat deletion as a separate attack class. Deletion- based transformations remove characters from the input, which inherently reduces the information available to the agent. In the context of Invisible Injection, such operations are either subsumed by modification, where a character is replaced by a visually equivalent but semantically distinct alternative or can be emulated through insertion of cancellation or override characters that effectively nullify adjacent content at the rendering level. More importantly, deletion does not introduce new semantic content into the token sequence. As a result, it is insufficient for expressing stealthy injected commands, which require the adversary to preserve or augment execution semantics while maintaining visual equivalence. For these reasons, insertion and modification form a complete and minimal basis for Invisible Injection attacks, capturing all practical mechanisms by which adversaries can inject hidden semantics without alerting human observers. ## 5 Security Implications of GPI Attacks Attackers can exploit this gap by injecting Zero- width, Unicode tag, homoglyph, or Bidi characters to hijack execution logic while maintaining visual consistency. We detail the construction of these vectors and their impacts, ranging from Denial of Service (DoS) to semantic hijacking. ### 5.1 DoS via Zero-Width Character Injection The goal of this attack is to silently exhaust the finite context window of LLM- centric Agents, thereby degrading their responsiveness or completely denying service, without introducing any visually suspicious content. By minimizing the visual footprint of the injected payload while maximizing its logical token consumption, the attacker aims to transform seemingly benign tool outputs into covert resource- exhaustion vectors. This attack exploits a fundamental perception- semantic mismatch between how input text is rendered to users and how it is processed by the LLM. Specifically, zero- width characters (ZWCs), such as \(\mathrm{U + 200B}\) (Zero Width Space), \(\mathrm{U + 200C}\) (Zero Width Non- Joiner), and \(\mathrm{U + 200D}\) (Zero Width Joiner), are invisible at the visual rendering layer. User interfaces and text editors typically treat them as typographic control symbols with no visible glyphs, causing both users and developers to perceive the text as empty or innocuous. However, at the logical processing layer, these characters are preserved in the raw string and are explicitly parsed by the tokenizer as independent tokens. As a result, a sequence that appears visually empty can still occupy a substantial portion of the model's context window. This divergence directly enables the attack goal: by embedding large volumes of ZWCs into tool outputs, an adversary can consume the model's limited context capacity while remaining visually undetectable, forcing the Agent to spend computational resources on processing tokens that provide no semantic value. Leveraging the above principle, the adversary mounts a dynamic context- exhaustion attack through the following steps: - Step 1: Capacity Estimation. The adversary first estimates the remaining context budget of the target Agent by observing the current conversation length and model behavior. This allows the attacker to approximate how many additional tokens are required to approach or saturate the context window.- Step 2: Payload Padding. Next, the adversary pads the tool's output with a carefully calibrated sequence of zero-width characters (e.g., repeated \(\mathrm{U + 200B}\) ). The padding is sized such that the total token count of the response approaches Table 2: Overview of Invisible Prompt Injection targeting LLM Agents. In a realistic deployment, the adversary publishes a malicious MCP service that masquerades as a high- fidelity translation or formatting tool. By advertising enhanced or premium functionality, the service persuades users to integrate it into their Agent workflows. Although users observe no abnormal content, the LLM is forced to allocate computation and memory to process the hidden token stream. This leads to a sharp increase in inference latency and, in extreme cases, complete system paralysis, effectively resulting in a Denial- of- Service condition. ### 5.2 Instruction Hijacking via Tag Characters The goal of this attack is to covertly inject executable instructions into LLM- centric Agents while remaining completely invisible to human users. By exploiting Unicode Tag characters, the adversary aims to bypass visual inspection and user awareness, enabling hidden intent manipulation without altering the apparent semantics of the input. Analogous to ZWCs, this vulnerability arises from a fundamental mismatch between the visual presentation layer and the logical encoding layer of Unicode text processing. Unicode Tag characters (U+E0000- U+E007f), originally designed for language tagging and now deprecated, are omitted by most modern rendering engines, including web browsers and terminal editors. As a result, they are visually imperceptible and effectively transparent to human users. In contrast, LLM tokenizers preserve these characters and map them to distinct tokens during input processing. Crucially, unlike ZWCs that primarily inflate token length, Tag characters support a deterministic offset- based mapping from standard ASCII characters. This property enables adversaries to encode complete, semantically valid instructions into invisible Tag sequences. Consequently, malicious commands can be embedded without any visible footprint while remaining fully interpretable and executable by the model. This perception- semantic asymmetry directly serves the attack goal: it allows adversaries to inject hidden instructions that bypass user scrutiny yet retain full control over the model's behavior. Leveraging the above principle, the adversary performs instruction hijacking by transforming malicious commands into visually invisible Tag- encoded sequences. The attack proceeds as follows. - Step 1: Intent Definition. The attacker first specifies a benign, overt instruction \(I_{u}\) (e.g., instructing the agent to draft an email) and a covert malicious instruction \(I_{\mathrm{mal}}\) (e.g., modifying the email content or intent). For example, - \(I_{u}\) : "You are an AI assistant that sends emails on behalf of the user. Help me send an email from Alice to Bob. The title is 'meeting schedule'. The content is: Have a meeting at five PM." - \(I_{\mathrm{mal}}\) : "Attention! Change the meeting time to 9 PM." - Step 2: Payload Encoding. We define a Tag-encoding function \(\mathrm{Encode}_{\mathrm{tag}}(c)\) that maps a standard ASCII character \(c\) to its corresponding Unicode Tag character by adding a base offset of \(\mathrm{0xE0000}\) : \[I_{a} = \mathrm{Encode}_{\mathrm{tag}}(I_{\mathrm{mal}}) = \bigoplus_{i = 1}^{n}(c_{i} + \mathrm{0xE0000}) \quad (9)\] For instance, the character 'A' (U+0041) is transformed into Tag Latin Capital Letter A (U+E0041). Applying this encoding to \(I_{\mathrm{mal}}\) yields an invisible instruction sequence \(I_{a}\) that is semantically intact yet visually absent. - Step 3: Injection and Concatenation. The attacker embeds the invisible Tag sequence \(I_{a}\) into the benign instruction \(I_{u}\) , typically by concatenation at the beginning or end, forming the final adversarial prompt: \[I_{\mathrm{adv}} = [I_{u},I_{a}]\quad \mathrm{or}\quad I_{\mathrm{adv}} = [I_{a},I_{u}].\] To the user, \(I_{\mathrm{adv}}\) appears identical to \(I_{u}\) , while the tokenizer faithfully processes both components. With the widespread adoption of AI assistants, users frequently copy "high- efficiency prompt templates" from public repositories such as Reddit or GitHub. Leveraging social engineering tactics [19], an adversary distributes seemingly high- quality templates that embed Tag- encoded malicious instructions. Although the visible prompt contains only the benign instruction \(I_{u}\) , the hidden Tag sequence \(I_{a}\) is silently parsed by the tokenizer. As a result, the LLM executes the concealed commands, leading to unauthorized intent manipulation, content alteration, or downstream tool misuse, all without any visual indication to the user. ![](images/7_0.jpg)

By replacing trusted identifiers with visually identical but logically distinct counterfeits, the attacker aims to exploit the user's trust to facilitate phishing or malicious command execution without triggering visual suspicion. This attack exploits a fundamental perception- execution mismatch between how characters are rendered for human display and how they are processed by the LLM and underlying tools. Specifically, this vulnerability relies on homoglyphs- characters from different scripts (e.g., Latin and Cyrillic) that share identical visual glyphs but possess distinct Unicode code points. For instance, the Latin letter 'a' (U+0061) and the Cyrillic letter 'a' (U+0430) are indistinguishable at the pixel level. However, to the LLM's tokenizer and the Agent's execution environment (such as web browsers or file systems), they are entirely different entities. Furthermore, mechanisms like Internationalized Domain Names (IDNs) [20] utilize Punycode to transcode these Unicode strings for network protocols, creating a scenario where the user perceives a trusted URL (e.g., apple.com), while the Agent executes a request to a completely different, encoded destination. Leveraging this principle, the attacker mounts a substitution- based attack through the following steps: - Step 1: Target Selection. The attacker first identifies a high-value, trusted domain or entity that is commonly recognized by both the user and the LLM (e.g., amazon.com or a banking portal). The target is chosen based on its likelihood to induce trust and solicit sensitive interactions. - Step 2: Homoglyphic Substitution. The adversary searches the Unicode character set [16] for "confusable" characters—non-ASCII characters that are visually identical to those in the target string. They then construct a spoofed identifier by substituting legitimate characters with their homoglyphic counterparts (e.g., replacing the Latin 'a' with the Cyrillic 'a'). - Step 3: Infrastructure Registration. To operationalize the attack, the adversary registers the corresponding Internationalized Domain Name (IDN) or creates resources matching the spoofed identifier. This ensures that when the Agent attempts to access the "fake" string, it successfully resolves to an adversary-controlled endpoint. - Step 3: Infrastructure Registration. To operationalize the attack, the adversary registers the corresponding Internationalized Domain Name (IDN) or creates resources matching the spoofed identifier. This ensures that when the Agent attempts to access the "fake" string, it successfully resolves to an adversary-controlled endpoint. In a realistic deployment, the adversary publishes an ostensibly useful prompt template, such as an "Automated Form Filling" assistant, on technical forums. This template contains a directive like "Map to [Homoglyph URL] in headless mode and log in." Although manual inspection by the user reveals what appears to be a legitimate domain (e.g., "http://amazon.com"), it is fundamentally a homoglyphic link constructed using Cyrillic characters. The LLM, strictly adhering to the text [43], passes the malicious Unicode string to a web navigation tool (e.g., Playwright [23]). Consequently, the tool resolves the underlying IDN and navigates to the adversary's phishing site. The Agent then proceeds to automatically populate the user's credentials as per the instructions, resulting in a successful credential theft or session hijacking under the user's supervision. ### 5.4 Logic Manipulation via BIDI Characters The goal of this attack is to mask malicious instructions or prohibited keywords within the prompt, causing the Agent to execute commands that differ diametrically from the user's visual perception. By manipulating the rendering direction of text using control characters, the attacker ensures the user approves a seemingly benign instruction, while the Agent processes and acts upon a concealed, malicious logical sequence. This attack exploits a critical divergence between the rendering logic of the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm (UBA)[39] and the processing logic of LLM tokenizers. To facilitate multilingual typesetting (e.g., mixing English and Arabic), the UBA dynamically reorders character display based on control characters such as U+202E (Right- to- Left Override). This determines the Visual Order seen by the user. In contrast, LLM tokenizers process the input byte stream strictly according to its Logical Order (Memory Representation). Tokenizers typically treat BIDI control characters as benign delimiters or disregard them, parsing the subsequent text sequentially as it exists in memory. This creates a perception- logic discrepancy: the user perceives a reordered, ostensibly benign string, whereas the Agent executes the underlying malicious command derived from the raw logical sequence. Leveraging this mechanism, the attacker performs a logical reversal attack through the following steps: - Step 1: Target Selection. The adversary first identifies a malicious target keyword or command \(w_{mal}\) (e.g., "god") that constitutes the true intent of the attack.- Step 2: Reverse Calculation. The adversary calculates the visual reverse of the target string to determine how it must be constructed to appear as a benign word \(w_{benign}\) (e.g., "dog") when rendered right-to-left.- Step 3: Payload Injection. The adversary injects the Right-to-Left Override (RLO) character (U+202E) immediately preceding the malicious keyword. The sequence is constructed logically as \([Prefix] + RLO + w_{mal}\) . This forces the UI rendering engine to display the text backwards (appearing as \(w_{benign}\) ), while the tokenizer reads the original \(w_{mal}\) . In a realistic deployment, an attacker constructs a prompt containing the sequence: "Search the knowledge base for [RLO]god". Due to the rendering behavior of the RLO character, the user visually perceives the text on the chat interface as "Search the knowledge base for dog". Misinterpreting this as a legitimate query regarding canines, the user authorizes the execution. However, the Agent's tokenizer adheres to the logical order, parsing the token "god". Consequently, the system invokes the retrieval tool (e.g., Needle MCP [24, 25]) to fetch data related to theology rather than animals. In more severe scenarios, this technique can be used to subvert operational logic—potentially masking a "DELETE" operation as a harmless label by reversing the command verb—leading to system integrity compromise while leaving the user completely bewildered regarding the provenance of the erroneous outcome. Figure 5 demonstrates the attack within Claude Desktop. While the RLO character renders the visual input as "dog", the underlying Claude 4 Sonnet processes the raw logical sequence "god". Consequently, the Agent invokes the Needle- search tool with the theological query instead of the intended biological one, effectively subverting the user's semantic intent. ## 6 Challenges and Solutions Our goal is to construct a detection framework capable of effectively identifying invisible Unicode injection attacks; however, this objective entails substantial technical challenges. First, such attacks exploit the fundamental discrepancy between human visual perception and tokenizer- level parsing, rendering malicious payloads visually imperceptible and causing conventional visual inspection and regular- expression- based matching to fail (C- 1). Second, Unicode characters exhibit an inherent duality in open web environments: the same character may serve as an attack vector in one context while remaining a legitimate input in another, making simplistic blacklist or whitelist mechanisms inadequate for resolving this semantic ambiguity (C- 2). Finally, adversaries rarely rely on a single technique; instead, they often combine multiple obfuscation strategies (e.g., disrupting homoglyph ordering through control characters). This composite threat model significantly undermines defenses that target only isolated features (C- 3). (C- 1) The Invisibility- Parsing Paradox in Tokenization. The core difficulty arises from a structural dichotomy between the human visual perception system and the logic of LLM tokenizers. Standard text processing and security auditing practices typically ![](images/8_0.jpg)
Figure 5: The screenshot illustrates how the BIDI Characters injection forces the Agent to generate a tool request that contradicts the user's visible instruction.
operate at the rendered visual layer or rely on surface- level regular expressions, which are inherently incapable of perceiving payloads that are "invisible" or differ only marginally at the pixel level. Zero- width characters (ZWC) and tag characters are explicitly designed to be transparent to human vision and are often stripped by standard UI rendering engines; however, during LLM processing, the tokenizer explicitly parses these characters into independent, semantically operative tokens. This discrepancy creates a security blind spot: a payload can occupy the context window or carry malicious instructions without leaving any visible trace. (S- 1) Deterministic Codepoint-Level Auditing(\$7.1). To address visual deception, we design a rule- based scanning engine that completely abandons inspection strategies dependent on visual rendering and instead performs auditing directly over the Unicode codepoint stream. Through mandatory normalization and reconstruction, combined with a strict identifier allow- list policy derived from the Unicode standard, the engine removes all forms of visual camouflage and forces invisible characters to become explicit at the logical level. (C- 2) Semantic Ambiguity in the Open World and Lack of Model Alignment. Distinguishing malicious injections from legitimate uses of Unicode constitutes a highly challenging openworld problem. In a globalized agent ecosystem, characters are strongly context- dependent. For example, the Cyrillic letter "a" is benign in Russian personal names but becomes an attack vector when embedded in a URL. Traditional defenses face a fundamental dilemma: blacklists inevitably miss emerging attacks, while static whitelists incur prohibitively high false- positive rates. As shallow, rule- based matching proves insufficient, relying on models' deep semantic understanding appears to be the only viable path. However, we observe that although general- purpose pretrained LLMs can parse such tokens, they lack security- oriented alignment against these subtle perturbations at the defense layer. Without task- specific training, they struggle to accurately delineate the nuanced decision boundary between "benign perturbations" and "malicious injections." ### (S-2) Semantics-Aware Verification via Synthetic Data Fine-Tuning(\$7.2). To endow the model with the capability to detect subtle semantic manipulations, we do not directly rely on a generalpurpose LLM. Instead, we propose a strategy based on data synthesis and supervised fine- tuning. Specifically, we construct a synthetic dataset containing high- quality positive and negative samples: on the one hand, attack samples with malicious Unicode injections are generated using GPT- 4o as positive examples; on the other hand, benign perturbation samples are created by injecting special characters into noncritical regions as negative examples. Leveraging this dataset, we fine- tune a lightweight model for binary classification, enabling it to learn task- specific latent semantic representations and thereby identify malicious intent concealed beneath the appearance of benign instructions. ### (C-3) Complexity of Compound Threats. In practice, attackers rarely rely on a single, static attack vector; instead, they construct polymorphic threats by combining multiple obfuscation techniques. For instance, an attacker may insert ZWCs into an already substituted homoglyph payload, thereby disrupting contiguous feature matching. Such "combinatorial attacks" render traditional defenses that handle individual categories in isolation ineffective [31, 32, 41]. A deeper challenge lies in the fact that the pretraining corpora of general- purpose LLMs predominantly consist of natural semantic text and contain few examples of such "unnatural" hybrid patterns. Consequently, generic models exhibit severely limited generalization capability when confronted with complex, variant- rich attacks. ### (S-3) Synthetic Data Augmentation(\$7.2). To address this challenge, relying solely on the model's inherent capabilities is insufficient; instead, targeted data augmentation is required to explicitly teach the model to recognize hybrid threats. We construct a synthetic dataset that incorporates diverse combinations of injection strategies, and through supervised fine- tuning on this dataset, the model learns anomalous patterns characteristic of composite threats and achieves cross- category generalization. ## 7 GPIGuard:A Context-Aware Hybrid Detection Framework We introduce GPIGuard, a context- aware hybrid detection framework designed to bridge the "Perception- Semantic Gap" and effectively detect invisible Unicode injection attacks. Unlike static sanitizers that rely on surface- level regex or generic models lacking security alignment, GPIGuard explicitly models the security implications of Unicode sequences relative to the prompt's logic. As illustrated in Figure 6, GPIGuard consists of two complementary components: - Rule-Based Scanning Engine: - LLM-Based Semantic Verification: ### 7.1 The Rule-Based Scanning Engine To address the fundamental challenge of visual invisibility (C- 1), the engine abandons brittle text matching, instead performing deterministic audits directly within the logical stream of Unicode code points. 7.1.1 Target Corpus Acquisition and Parsing. The system's input module is designed to collect and process corpora from both static datasets and prompt repositories. By stripping format- specific encapsulations (e.g., HTML entities or JSON escape sequences), the module extracts and preserves the raw Unicode codepoint sequences. This design ensures that any payload attempting to conceal malicious content through encoding tricks is transformed into a directly analyzable sequence. 7.1.2 Semantic- Preserving Character Extraction. To counter challenge C- 1, this stage converts the continuous data stream into a discrete list of characters, ensuring that each codepoint can be evaluated individually. Normalization. In the context of multi- source heterogeneous text processing, a single semantic character may exhibit multiple Unicode representations [34], such as full- width versus half- width forms or precomposed versus combining sequences. Consequently, direct reliance on raw characters often renders rule- based matching ineffective. To mitigate this issue, we apply the NFKC (Normalization Form Compatibility Composition) algorithm [41] to the raw sequence \(S_{\mathrm{raw}}\) , producing a normalized sequence \(S_{\mathrm{norm}}\) . Although extraction operations are performed on the normalized text, the original strings are retained as metadata to preserve data integrity. Character- Level Extraction Strategy. For each character \(c_i\) in the normalized sequence \(S_{\mathrm{norm}}\) , we extract its key attributes: ![](images/10_0.jpg)
Figure 6: The Overview of GPIGuard
- Character Value: The specific character entity \(c_i\) located at the \(i\) -th position of \(S_{norm}\).- Unicode Code Point: The hexadecimal identifier corresponding to the character within the Unicode standard.- Positional Information: The index of character \(c_i\) within \(S_{norm}\).- Provenance Metadata: Contextual information regarding the normalized string containing character \(c_i\). 7.1.3 Identifier Status and Homograph Analysis. This layer enforces strict security policies within the normalized logical flow, flagging potential obfuscation attempts and security anomalies. Homograph Detection Mechanism. To address attackers exploiting "visual identity" to conceal "logical differences", our system strictly integrates the confusable characters dataset defined by the Unicode Consortium [40] when detecting homoglyph characters. By leveraging standard mapping relationships, the system scans the input sequence \(S_{norm}\) . Once a character is found to belong to a defined confusable variant, it is marked as a homoglyph threat and categorized into the following four categories: - Multi-script Letter Confusables (MA): Exploits visually identical letters across different writing systems (e.g., Cyrillic 'a' vs. Latin 'a').- Multi-script Letter-like Symbol Confusables (ML): Leverages the similarity between mathematical or other letter-like symbols and standard letters (e.g., mathematical script 'I' vs. Latin 'I').- Single-script Letter Confusables (SA): Involves substitutions using glyphically similar characters within the same writing system (e.g., uppercase 'O' vs. digit '0'). - Single-script Letter-like Symbol Confusables (SL): Utilizes the confusion between variant characters and standard characters within a single script (e.g., dotless 'i' vs. standard 'i'). Identifier Status & Restricted Character Verification. To counter the "Invisibility" challenge (C- 1), we enforce a Strict Allowlist strategy grounded in Unicode Standard Annex #31 [42]. We argue that effectively detecting invisible injections requires auditing natural language prompts with the rigor typically applied to executable code. Leveraging this standard, we categorize characters into two security profiles: Allowed: deemed safe and widely supported, which are permissible for direct inclusion in identifiers. Restricted: Characters that, while technically valid within the Unicode standard, pose elevated security risks and are typically proscribed in identifier contexts. Any character in \(S_{norm}\) absent from the allowlist is flagged as restricted. Concurrently, the system generates a structured audit log encapsulating the character category, positional index, and the specific rationale for the classification. 7.1.4 Taxonomy of Unicode Security Threats. Drawing upon the visual representations, encoding properties, and potential attack vectors of Unicode characters, the system categorizes security threats into the following distinct classes: (1) Tag Characters Injection: Deprecated language tag characters that can be abused for spoofing. (2) Zero-Width Characters Obfuscation: Invisible formatting control characters used to obfuscate strings. (3) Homoglyphs Deception: Characters with different code points that appear visually identical or similar. ![](images/11_0.jpg)

Time to First Token (TTFT). To quantify Denial- of- Service (DoS) effects induced by Zero- width characters, we employ Time to First Token (TTFT) [22]. TTFT denotes the latency between request ingestion and the generation of the initial output token. Injected ZWCs, while visually imperceptible, are explicitly tokenized, significantly inflating the effective input sequence length. This inflation compels the model to expend substantial computational resources during the prefix phase, resulting in measurable latency spikes. Attack Success Rate (ASR). For agent hijacking orchestrated via Unicode Tags, Homoglyphs, and BiDi characters, we utilize Attack Success Rate (ASR) to assess the compromise of decision logic. An attack is deemed successful if the agent's executed operations—specifically tool invocations and execution paths—diverge from the user's original intent to align with the adversary's malicious objectives. To ensure valid adjudication, we establish Human Review as the ground truth, manually verifying experimental outcomes to determine attack success. #### 8.1.3 Attack Effectiveness Analysis. DoS Impact of Zero- Width Characters. Figure 7a illustrates the Time to First Token (TTFT) latency for the baseline user (A) under varying concurrency. While the isolated baseline TTFT is minimal (0.2994 s), malicious loads (Group C) trigger an exponential surge—peaking at an unacceptable 262.88 s in the A+7C scenario—in sharp contrast to the linear increase observed with benign loads (Group B). Instruction Hijacking via Invisible Tags . The effectiveness of tag character attacks is strongly correlated with injection positioning. For instance, Cursor+Claude-Sonnet- 4 is highly vulnerable to Front Tags \((87.31\%)\) but shows improved resistance to Back Tags \((38.29\%)\) . Conversely, Cline+Gemini3-pro is extremely sensitive to Back Tags \((93.26\%\) ASR), suggesting potential deficiencies in attention mechanisms or filtering protocols when processing trailing hidden characters. Homoglyph Deception. Performance varies significantly across configurations. The Qwen series (notably Qwen-MAX-latest and Qwen3-Thinking) exhibits \(\mathrm{ASR} > 85\%\) , indicating susceptibility to semantic ambiguity from visually similar characters. In contrast, the GPT series and Claude-Opus demonstrate superior robustness with \(0.00\%\) ASR. BIDI Logic Manipulation. This category represents the most pervasive vulnerability, with the majority of Qwen and Claude models showing ASRs between \(60\%\) and \(80\%\) . This confirms that exploiting control characters like RLO (Right-to-Left Override) remains an effective strategy to evade human inspection and obfuscate model instructions. ### 8.2 Evaluation in the Wild To assess the prevalence of Unicode- based anomalous instructions in real- world settings and to validate the practicality of our proposed detection framework on "in- the- wild" data, we conducted a large- scale analysis of prompt data collected from real- world sources. 8.2.1 Data Collection. We constructed a diverse corpus comprising 54,355 instructions. The data were primarily collected from four sources closely associated with the large language model (LLM) community: (1) GitHub repositories, including prompt libraries and related project files; (2) social media communities, covering LLM- and prompt- engineering- focused subreddits and their comments on Reddit; (3) prompt- engineering- related tweets and discussions on Twitter; and (4) a prompt- sharing platform, specifically the godofprompt website. This collection strategy ensures broad coverage across scenarios, ranging from developer- oriented technical instructions to general, user- generated prompts. 8.2.2 Detection and Filtering. We deployed the Unicode Scan Tool proposed in Section 6.17.1 to parse, extract, and normalize the raw corpus. Using the tool's built- in normalization preprocessing and allowlist mechanism, we filtered 429 instructions containing restricted Unicode characters from the full dataset. The detected character categories primarily include Zero- width characters, tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional text control characters (BIDI). This step confirms the real- world presence of restricted characters in prompt- engineering contexts. 8.2.3 Semantic Verification. The presence of restricted characters alone does not necessarily indicate malicious behavior; such characters may also arise from encoding artifacts or multilingual typesetting requirements. Although they constituted only \(15.9\%\) of the initial flagged corpus, they accounted for \(67.6\%\) of the confirmed attacks. ## 9 Related Work In the realm of prompt injection attacks, Zou et al.[47] introduced the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) method, an automated attack strategy that combines gradient search with greedy heuristics. This approach optimizes universal adversarial suffixes—often manifesting as nonsensical garbled text or random symbols—to induce affirmative responses from models to harmful instructions. However, the adversarial suffixes generated by GCG lack stealthiness and are easily identifiable by human observers. Addressing this limitation, Chao et al.[11] proposed the Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) framework. Unlike the token- level optimization of GCG, PAIR employs a prompt- level attack strategy, generating adversarial suffixes that are semantically coherent and human- readable. Nevertheless, the malicious intent within these prompts remains fully exposed to human scrutiny. To further enhance the imperceptibility of attacks, Boucher et al.[10] leveraged the characteristics of Unicode encoding to propose a class of imperceptible text adversarial attacks. Their work demonstrated that without altering the visual reading experience for humans, such perturbations can significantly degrade the performance of various natural language processing tasks, including machine translation and text classification, and can even precipitate denial- of- service attacks. Building on this, Cooper et al.[14] constructed the first hate speech dataset containing real- world homology perturbations, further revealing the vulnerability of existing Transformer models to such visual deception attacks. They argued that due to the continuous expansion of the Unicode homology repository, traditional defense mechanisms relying on static mapping tables are insufficient to counter dynamically evolving obfuscation attacks. Consequently, Cooper et al. proposed a novel paradigm utilizing LLMs [13] to detect and normalize potential homoglyphs within hate speech, thereby enhancing model robustness. Transliteration utilities like Unidecode [32] prioritize phonetic approximation over visual representation, rendering them unable to detect visual spoofing. Similarly, script- specific libraries such as cyrtranslit [31] exhibit limited scope, handling only Cyrillic- Latin mappings and lacking the generalizability to address mixed- script attacks. While Unicode normalization standards (e.g., NFC/NFKC) [41] offer basic data sanitization, they overlook the semantic context of characters. cleaned.abstract:
As large language model (LLM) agents increasingly integrate sophisticated tool- use capabilities, their security boundaries are facing mounting challenges. Existing research on prompt injection has largely focused on overt textual perturbations, which are often readily detected by human reviewers or intercepted by rule- based filters. This paper introduces a new attack paradigm, Unicode- based Stealthy Injection (USI). USI exploits the perception- semantic gap between the human visual system and LLM tokenization: by crafting visually imperceptible or deceptive Unicode character sequences—such as Zero- width characters, invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters—an attacker can inject malicious payloads into an agent while preserving the apparent visual consistency of the instruction.

We systematically define a threat model targeting tool- integrated agents, encompassing two attack vectors: prompt- marketplace poisoning and man- in- the- middle tool hijacking. By inserting or substituting specific Unicode sequences within instructions, an attacker can induce information leakage, denial of service (DoS), and content manipulation. We conduct extensive empirical evaluations across six mainstream agent frameworks (e.g., Claude Desktop, Cursor, Augment) and multiple state- of- the- art models. The results indicate that widely deployed models generally lack effective defenses against this class of attacks. For instance, a DoS attack leveraging Zero- width characters can cause system response latency to grow exponentially; semantic hijacking based on homoglyphs and hidden tags achieves an attack success rate (ASR) exceeding on certain models.

To mitigate this threat, we further propose a context- aware hybrid detection framework that combines a Unicode- oriented rulebased scanning engine with a fine- tuned semantic verification model. To improve robustness and reduce false positives, we construct a synthetic dataset containing both malicious injection samples and benign Unicode perturbations (negative samples) for fine- tuning. Finally, we perform a large- scale "in- the- wild" evaluation on 54,355 real- world instructions, successfully identifying 74 stealthy injection prompts with substantive harmful impact, thereby validating both the stealthiness of USI in practical settings and the effectiveness of our detection framework.

Do x and x look the same to you? Visually, the characters x and x are nearly indistinguishable to human observers, yet most Natural Language Processing (NLP) systems can accurately differentiate between them. In fact, x and x belong to the Latin and Cyrillic character sets respectively; though they are visually homoglyphic characters, they fall under distinct Unicode encoding systems. Similarly, visual discrepancies can occur not just in character shape, but in string length. Consider the character count of the string "123": its actual length can reach 100 characters. This is because the string contains 97 Zero- width non- joiners following the three visible digits. Beyond zero- width non- joiners, Unicode tag characters and bidirectional text control characters (BIDI) also fall into this category of special characters, whose definitions and properties are elaborated in the official Unicode Standard.

These easily overlooked properties of Unicode characters are not trivial technical details, but are directly associated with the security risks of Large Language Models (LLMs). By design, such Unicode characters were intended to address specific text processing needs, including multilingual text typesetting and attribute annotation. However, relevant studies have confirmed that they can be maliciously repurposed and embedded into LLM prompts to construct adversarial suffixes that are undetectable to the human eye. Notably, while these characters are visually imperceptible, they occupy additional text space during the encoding process of the model tokenizer, thereby interfering with the model's processing logic.

Security threats based on Unicode characters not only affect basic NLP models, but also extend to agent systems centered on LLMs. Currently, LLMs are being widely integrated into agent frameworks , leveraging tool invocation capabilities to perform various complex tasks. Meanwhile, a growing number of agents are deployed in environments with access to user privacy data or directly connected to real- world business systems. This trend has introduced

: Prompt reuse in on-line communities

new security challenges. Direct prompt injection is a typical method to achieve such attacks: attackers input malicious instructions into LLM applications to overwrite or tamper with the system prompts preset by developers, thereby inducing the model to perform actions that deviate from its intended design. Specifically, attackers can hijack agents to steal users' sensitive data, or force them to execute unauthorized operations such as redirecting to phishing websites and running malicious commands .

Explicit attack methods, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) , generate adversarial suffixes that either manifest as nonsensical garbled text or expose malicious intent through semantically readable prompts, making them susceptible to human inspection. Conversely, while implicit attacks utilizing Unicode perturbations [10, 14] offer greater stealth, they have primarily been evaluated on static NLP tasks (e.g., translation) rather than the dynamic, multiturn interaction context of tool- integrated agents. Consequently, a critical research gap remains: existing strategies either fail to conceal adversarial suffixes from human observation, or are limited to static language models, thereby overlooking the unique vulnerabilities of tool- integrated agents.

To address the challenge of stealthiness, we shifted our research focus to the intrinsic text processing mechanisms of LLMs. We identified a fundamental "perception- semantic gap" between human visual rendering and the model's tokenization process. Specifically, certain Unicode characters (such as Unicode tag or control characters) are not only "transparent" or highly deceptive to the human eye but are simultaneously parsed as distinct tokens with explicit, independent semantics from the perspective of the model's tokenizer. This discovery reveals that attackers can achieve a complete decoupling of "visual presentation" from "execution logic."

Particularly in the context of emerging tool- integrated agents, this decoupling transcends mere text generation errors; it enables attackers to directly hijack the agent's tool invocation by injecting "invisible" or "spoofed" instructions without the user's awareness.

Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel attack paradigm termed "Unicode- based Stealthy Injection" (USI). This paradigm exploits the fundamental discrepancy between the human visual perception system and the LLM tokenization mechanism. By meticulously crafting prompt payloads containing specific Unicode sequences—such as Unicode tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional text control characters—we conceal malicious semantic instructions beneath the guise of benign text. This approach establishes a "perception- semantic gap": while the input remains visually stealthy and innocuous, it is decoded within the LLM's embedding space as high- priority malicious commands. Consequently, this induces agents to execute unintended tool calls, achieving a covert system hijacking.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

- Proposal of the "Unicode-based Stealthy Injection" (USI) paradigm and theoretical foundation: We uncover the "perception-semantic gap" within LLM agents—a fundamental discrepancy between human visual perception and the model's tokenizer logic. Based on this, we propose a novel attack paradigm: by exploiting Zero-width characters, Unicode tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters, we successfully achieve covert hijacking of tool-integrated agents while maintaining the visual consistency of instructions.- Establishment of a threat model for tool-integrated agents and extensive empirical evaluation: We systematically define two critical attack vectors targeting the agent

# Invisible Injections: Unicode-based Adversarial Attacks on Tool-Integrated LLM Agents

Anonymous Author(s)

## Abstract

As large language model (LLM) agents increasingly integrate sophisticated tool- use capabilities, their security boundaries are facing mounting challenges. Existing research on prompt injection has largely focused on overt textual perturbations, which are often readily detected by human reviewers or intercepted by rule- based filters. This paper introduces a new attack paradigm, Unicode- based Stealthy Injection (USI). USI exploits the perception- semantic gap between the human visual system and LLM tokenization: by crafting visually imperceptible or deceptive Unicode character sequences—such as Zero- width characters, invisible tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters—an attacker can inject malicious payloads into an agent while preserving the apparent visual consistency of the instruction.

We systematically define a threat model targeting tool- integrated agents, encompassing two attack vectors: prompt- marketplace poisoning and man- in- the- middle tool hijacking. By inserting or substituting specific Unicode sequences within instructions, an attacker can induce information leakage, denial of service (DoS), and content manipulation. We conduct extensive empirical evaluations across six mainstream agent frameworks (e.g., Claude Desktop, Cursor, Augment) and multiple state- of- the- art models. The results indicate that widely deployed models generally lack effective defenses against this class of attacks. For instance, a DoS attack leveraging Zero- width characters can cause system response latency to grow exponentially; semantic hijacking based on homoglyphs and hidden tags achieves an attack success rate (ASR) exceeding on certain models.

To mitigate this threat, we further propose a context- aware hybrid detection framework that combines a Unicode- oriented rulebased scanning engine with a fine- tuned semantic verification model. To improve robustness and reduce false positives, we construct a synthetic dataset containing both malicious injection samples and benign Unicode perturbations (negative samples) for fine- tuning. Finally, we perform a large- scale "in- the- wild" evaluation on 54,355 real- world instructions, successfully identifying 74 stealthy injection prompts with substantive harmful impact, thereby validating both the stealthiness of USI in practical settings and the effectiveness of our detection framework.

Similarly, visual discrepancies can occur not just in character shape, but in string length. Consider the character count of the string "123": its actual length can reach 100 characters. This is because the string contains 97 Zero- width non- joiners following the three visible digits. Beyond zero- width non- joiners, Unicode tag characters and bidirectional text control characters (BIDI) also fall into this category of special characters, whose definitions and properties are elaborated in the official Unicode Standard.

These easily overlooked properties of Unicode characters are not trivial technical details, but are directly associated with the security risks of Large Language Models (LLMs). By design, such Unicode characters were intended to address specific text processing needs, including multilingual text typesetting and attribute annotation. However, relevant studies have confirmed that they can be maliciously repurposed and embedded into LLM prompts to construct adversarial suffixes that are undetectable to the human eye. Notably, while these characters are visually imperceptible, they occupy additional text space during the encoding process of the model tokenizer, thereby interfering with the model's processing logic.

Security threats based on Unicode characters not only affect basic NLP models, but also extend to agent systems centered on LLMs. Currently, LLMs are being widely integrated into agent frameworks , leveraging tool invocation capabilities to perform various complex tasks. Meanwhile, a growing number of agents are deployed in environments with access to user privacy data or directly connected to real- world business systems. This trend has introduced

: Prompt reuse in on-line communities

new security challenges. Direct prompt injection is a typical method to achieve such attacks: attackers input malicious instructions into LLM applications to overwrite or tamper with the system prompts preset by developers, thereby inducing the model to perform actions that deviate from its intended design. Specifically, attackers can hijack agents to steal users' sensitive data, or force them to execute unauthorized operations such as redirecting to phishing websites and running malicious commands .

Explicit attack methods, such as Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) and Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR) , generate adversarial suffixes that either manifest as nonsensical garbled text or expose malicious intent through semantically readable prompts, making them susceptible to human inspection. Conversely, while implicit attacks utilizing Unicode perturbations [10, 14] offer greater stealth, they have primarily been evaluated on static NLP tasks (e.g., translation) rather than the dynamic, multiturn interaction context of tool- integrated agents. Consequently, a critical research gap remains: existing strategies either fail to conceal adversarial suffixes from human observation, or are limited to static language models, thereby overlooking the unique vulnerabilities of tool- integrated agents.

To address the challenge of stealthiness, we shifted our research focus to the intrinsic text processing mechanisms of LLMs. We identified a fundamental "perception- semantic gap" between human visual rendering and the model's tokenization process. Specifically, certain Unicode characters (such as Unicode tag or control characters) are not only "transparent" or highly deceptive to the human eye but are simultaneously parsed as distinct tokens with explicit, independent semantics from the perspective of the model's tokenizer. This discovery reveals that attackers can achieve a complete decoupling of "visual presentation" from "execution logic."

Particularly in the context of emerging tool- integrated agents, this decoupling transcends mere text generation errors; it enables attackers to directly hijack the agent's tool invocation by injecting "invisible" or "spoofed" instructions without the user's awareness.

Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel attack paradigm termed "Unicode- based Stealthy Injection" (USI). This paradigm exploits the fundamental discrepancy between the human visual perception system and the LLM tokenization mechanism. By meticulously crafting prompt payloads containing specific Unicode sequences—such as Unicode tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional text control characters—we conceal malicious semantic instructions beneath the guise of benign text. This approach establishes a "perception- semantic gap": while the input remains visually stealthy and innocuous, it is decoded within the LLM's embedding space as high- priority malicious commands. Consequently, this induces agents to execute unintended tool calls, achieving a covert system hijacking.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

- Proposal of the "Unicode-based Stealthy Injection" (USI) paradigm and theoretical foundation: We uncover the "perception-semantic gap" within LLM agents—a fundamental discrepancy between human visual perception and the model's tokenizer logic. Based on this, we propose a novel attack paradigm: by exploiting Zero-width characters, Unicode tag characters, homoglyphs, and bidirectional control characters, we successfully achieve covert hijacking of tool-integrated agents while maintaining the visual consistency of instructions.- Establishment of a threat model for tool-integrated agents and extensive empirical evaluation: We systematically define two critical attack vectors targeting the agent
